exemplar.title:
Investigating the effects of different damping mediums (air, water, and 
oil) on the parameters of a mass-spring system

detailedExemplarData.title:
Investigating the effects of different damping mediums (air, water, and 
oil) on the parameters of a mass-spring system

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Purpose and focus are consistently clear, with a wellâsignposted introduction and headings.
Research question is explicitly stated, tightly focused, and clearly linked to the analysis and conclusion.
Method section is generally complete, listing variables, materials, stepâbyâstep procedures and uncertainty estimates.
weaknesses: Methodology relies on manual timing and visual readings, introducing large random errors without justification.
No experimental validation or theoretical justification for treating water as a âcriticalâ damping medium.
Inconsistency in replicates: the critically damped condition lacks the same number of trials specified for other media.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The three strands average at just under 5/6. Communication and question formulation clearly meet the 5â6 band, but methodological limitations (large random errors, insufficient justification for critical damping, inconsistent replicates) prevent a topâband score. On balance, a score of 5 accurately reflects strong focus and method with room for improvement.

strengths: Solid conceptual understanding of SHM and damping regimes, with correct derivation and application of key equations.
Terminology is generally accurate and consistently applied (e.g., logarithmic decrement, quality factor).
weaknesses: Uncertainty propagation to final parameters is incomplete, limiting the depth of quantitative analysis.
Minor inconsistencies in notation (alternating use of b and c) and occasional typographical slips.
Lack of advanced dataâfitting techniques (e.g., nonâlinear regression) to extract decay constants.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Performance clearly sits within the 3â4 band: the student demonstrates good knowledge and appropriate use of concepts, but gaps in full uncertainty treatment and theoretical validation prevent entry into the top band. A score of 4 faithfully represents this level.

strengths: Data analysis is thorough, with clear calculation of averages, uncertainties, logarithmic decrements, damping coefficients and quality factors.
Argumentation is wellâdeveloped and coherent, linking observations directly to theoretical expectations.
Errors are identified and quantified, and reasonable improvements (video analysis, depth marks) are proposed.
weaknesses: Uncertainty propagation remains partial and no quantitative comparison with literature values is performed.
Classification of water as critically damped is assumed without experimental or theoretical confirmation.
Alternative influences (e.g., temperature effects on viscosity) are acknowledged but not fully explored.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although one strand (critical evaluation) sits at the top of the 4â6 band and the others in the 7â9 band, the overall critical engagement, quality of analysis and coherence across the discussion justify a highâend 7â9 band score. Assigning 9 acknowledges predominantly strong performance in all three strands with only minor gaps.

strengths: All required structural elements (title page, abstractâstyle introduction, TOC, headings, bibliography, page numbers, word count) are present and consistently formatted.
Figures, tables and graphs are wellâintegrated and colourâcoded to support clear comparison.
Layout supports easy navigation through the report.
weaknesses: Several images are lowâresolution and captions are sometimes misaligned.
Minor formatting inconsistencies (duplicated TOC entries, cramped equations) detract from overall polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets the descriptors of the top 3â4 band: structure and layout effectively support communication. Only small lapses in image quality and formatting prevent a flawless submission, so a score of 4 is appropriate.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key physics terms (logarithmic decrement, damping coefficient, quality factor, under/over/critical damping) are consistently defined and generally used correctly.  Equations are properly formatted and variables are explained.  Minor inconsistenciesâsuch as alternating use of b and c for the damping constant and a few typographical slipsâlimit full consistency, but accuracy of terminology is largely maintained.  Overall performance aligns with the descriptor for the 3-4 band and justifies a score of 4.

The student constructs a clear, stepwise argument linking observations (amplitude decay, period elongation) to the theoretical framework of damping.  Graphs and tables are used effectively to support points, and the conclusion synthesises multiple parameters to answer the research question.  Some assertionsâparticularly the classification of water as critically dampedâare taken at face value without quantitative confirmation, and potential alternative explanations (e.g., temperature effects on viscosity) are not fully explored.  Argumentation is therefore âwell-developedâ rather than âsophisticatedâ, warranting a 7 in the 7-9 band.

The student provides a generally sound procedure: clear list of variables, wellâlabelled materials, step-by-step method and five trials per medium.  Appropriate basic measurements (amplitudes, periods) and uncertainty estimates are included.  However, the choice of only visual/ruler readings and manual stopwatch timing introduces large random error; no justification is given for the small number of oscillations or for treating water as âcriticalâ.  More sophisticated data-logging or video analysis and a higher trial count would have strengthened reliability.  These limitations place the work at the top of the 3-4 band rather than the 5-6 band.

The explanation of SHM, damping regimes, logarithmic decrement, damping coefficient and quality factor shows solid conceptual understanding.  Relevant equations are derived or referenced correctly and applied to experimental data.  The discussion links theory to observed trends (e.g., viscosity vs. damping coefficient).  Nonetheless, some gaps remain: full propagation of uncertainties to final parameters is incomplete, advanced curve-fitting to extract decay constants is absent, and the assumption of critical damping in water is not theoretically validated.  These issues prevent a move into the 5-6 band but the work clearly exceeds the âlimitedâ level, meriting a strong 4 in the 3-4 band.

The report identifies random and systematic errors, quantifies key uncertainties and suggests reasonable improvements (e.g., video analysis, depth marks).  It comments on reliability by averaging trials and discussing trends.  Yet, evaluation remains largely descriptive; numerical impact of errors on final conclusions is limited, and the claim of âcritical dampingâ is not critically interrogated.  The work demonstrates âgood evaluation with some critical analysisâ, fitting the 4-6 band, with performance around the midpoint (5).

The title, abstract-style introduction and repeated signposting in headings communicate exactly what is being studied (massâspring oscillations under three damping media) and why it matters. The description is concise yet complete, and the focus never drifts from the central theme of damping effects. Minor duplication in the table-of-contents and occasional layout glitches weaken overall polish, hence a 5 rather than the full 6, but the work still reaches the âaccurately and effectively communicatedâ level described in the 5-6 band.

Figures, tables and graphs are numerous, labelled and integrated near relevant text; colour coding aids comparison.  Nevertheless, several images are low-resolution and some spacing/formatting inconsistencies (e.g., misaligned captions, duplicated TOC rows) detract from overall polish.  These minor issues place the work within the effective but not flawless presentation level, earning a 3 in the 3-4 band.

The research question â âHow do different damping conditions â¦ affect the amplitude, period, logarithmic decrement, damping coefficient and quality factor of a mass-spring system?â â is explicitly stated, tightly focused and revisited throughout data presentation, analysis and conclusion.  The linkage between question, hypothesis, data treatment and conclusion is evident.  A small weakness is the assumed equivalence of water with âcritical dampingâ, which is not justified experimentally, but this does not undermine the clarity or focus of the question itself.  Therefore the strand comfortably meets, but does not absolutely maximise, the 5-6 band.

Data are processed into averages, uncertainties, logarithmic decrements, damping coefficients and quality factors, each supported by sample calculations and graphs.  Analysis is logically connected to the research question, and trends across media are interpreted in the context of theoretical expectations.  However, the analysis stops short of higher-level treatment such as non-linear regression to extract decay constants or quantitative comparison with literature values; uncertainty propagation is partial.  The strength of the dataset and coherent interpretation place the work within the 7-9 band, but methodological depth does not reach the sophistication required for 9, hence a 7.

All required EE structural elements (title page, abstract-equivalent introduction, table of contents, headings, bibliography, page numbers, word count) are present and generally consistent.  Formatting is clear and supports navigation.  Minor lapsesâduplicate headings, occasional cramped equations and TOC punctuationâprevent a perfect presentation but the work still exemplifies âclear and appropriate structureâ consistent with the top band (3-4).  A score of 4 is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The quality factor graph should include labeled error bars for Qâ to enable evaluation of statistical significance in comparisons.
Works Cited entries vary in style (italicization, date format); adopt a consistent citation format (APA, MLA, etc.) to enhance professionalism.
The sentence introducing logarithmic decrement ends abruptly; ensure all explanatory text is complete to maintain coherence.
The exemplar uncertainty calculation for Î´â misapplies the square operations; review each algebraic step to ensure correct propagation of relative uncertainties.
The damping coefficient graph also lacks error bars, preventing assessment of overlap between conditions; include uncertainties to improve interpretation.
The error analysis table categorizes some errors but could be strengthened by quantifying each sourceâs effect on final parameters and linking to numerical uncertainty values.
Terminology is inconsistent: the damping constant is introduced as c in Equation 3 but later referred to as b. Standardize notation to avoid confusion in equations and text.
The inclusion of a precise word count at the top of the title page meets the formal requirement, demonstrating clear attention to formatting guidelines.
The raw data table is very dense and spans the full page height; consider splitting it or increasing font size and spacing to improve readability.
The uncertainty calculation for average amplitude omits propagation of the instrumentâs measurement error (Â± 0.05 cm); incorporate this to provide a more accurate uncertainty estimate.
Uncertainty in average period is calculated using maxâmin/2 but does not include the Â± 0.3 s timing resolution; include both systematic and random components when propagating uncertainty.

The assumption that water provides critical damping is stated without experimental or theoretical justification; consider measuring viscosity or comparing bÂ² and ÏâÂ² to validate this classification.
The dependent variable list cuts off midâsentence (ââ¦resistance offered by theâ); ensure each variable description is complete for clarity and completeness.

The procedure for the critically damped condition does not specify repeating trials five times as in other conditions; include consistent replicate instructions to strengthen methodological reliability.
The average amplitude graph lacks error bars, making it difficult to assess the significance of differences; add vertical error bars representing calculated uncertainties.
Rather than listing individual Î´n values, calculate and discuss an overall average logarithmic decrement to summarise damping behaviour more effectively.
The materials list includes âClamp Standâ twice; remove duplicate entries to improve the clarity and professionalism of the materials section.
Uncertainty in time measurements is assigned based on reaction time but omits the stopwatchâs intrinsic resolution; include instrument precision when estimating overall uncertainty.
The damping coefficients bââbâ appear rounded inconsistently with b = Î´/T; recheck each value and rounding convention for accuracy.
The natural angular frequency Ïâ is computed as âk rather than â(k/m); recalculate Q using Ïâ = â(k/m) to align with theoretical definitions.
Background and Overview headings are both H2 level; consider reorganizing to distinguish general background from the specific SHM overview for clearer structure.
The conclusion text is cut off midâsentence; ensure the final summary fully articulates how Q values confirm underdamped and overdamped classifications.
The Table of Contents contains duplicated rows and inconsistent indentation; streamline entries to improve navigation and presentation.

similarExemplars.title:
How does varying the initial angular displacement of a magnetic pendulum affect
its stopping time due to damping caused by the eddy currents induced in the copper
plates below it when the string used for the pendulum, the magnet used, the
dimensions of the copper plate, and the magnetic field strength of the magnet are
kept controlled?

userSubmission.researchQuestion:
How does varying the initial angular displacement of a magnetic pendulum affect
its stopping time due to damping caused by the eddy currents induced in the copper
plates below it when the string used for the pendulum, the magnet used, the
dimensions of the copper plate, and the magnetic field strength of the magnet are
kept controlled?
